strMeal: Lotus Root Soup with Pork Ribs
strInstructions: Rinse the pork ribs and blanch them briefly in boiling water to remove impurities. Drain and rinse well. In a clean pot, combine the ribs, sliced lotus root, dried shiitake mushrooms, peanuts, ginger, and enough water to cover. Bring to a boil, then reduce the heat and simmer until the pork is tender and the lotus root is slightly starchy but still firm. Season with salt near the end of cooking. Serve hot as a comforting soup.

strMeal: Steamed Pork Patty with Preserved Vegetables
strInstructions: Rinse the dong cai several times to remove excess salt, then squeeze it dry and chop finely. In a bowl, combine the chopped preserved vegetables with ground pork, water, Shaoxing wine, light soy sauce, white pepper, sesame oil, cornstarch, and sugar. Mix in one direction until the pork mixture becomes sticky and well combined. Spread the mixture evenly onto a shallow heatproof plate. Steam over high heat until the pork is fully cooked and juicy. Garnish with chopped scallions and serve hot with steamed rice.
searchAliases: steamed pork patty with preserved vegetables

strInstructions: Marinate pork pieces with soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, egg, salt, and cornstarch. Coat the pork with starch and fry until crisp and golden. Stir-fry bell peppers, onion, and pineapple briefly. Simmer ketchup, rice vinegar, sugar, soy sauce, water, and cornstarch into a glossy sweet-sour sauce. Toss the fried pork and vegetables in the sauce just before serving so the coating stays crisp.

Cut pork shoulder into long strips. Marinate with hoisin sauce, maltose or honey, soy sauce, dark soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, five spice powder, garlic, and red fermented bean curd if available. Refrigerate overnight. Roast on a rack, basting several times with the marinade and honey mixture. Continue roasting until the pork is glossy, caramelized, lightly charred, and cooked through. Rest before slicing.

Slice beef thinly and marinate with soy sauce, oil, cornstarch, and Shaoxing wine. Blanch or steam broccoli until bright green and tender-crisp. Sear beef quickly in a hot wok, then remove. Stir-fry garlic and ginger, add oyster sauce, soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, and stock. Return beef and broccoli to the wok and toss with cornstarch slurry until the sauce coats everything.

strMeal: Steamed Chicken with Mushrooms and Dried Lily Flowers
strInstructions: Rinse the dried wood ear mushrooms, dried lily flowers, and dried shiitake mushrooms, then soak them in hot water until fully rehydrated. Trim any tough mushroom stems and squeeze out excess water. Cut the chicken thighs into large bite-size pieces. In a bowl, combine the chicken, rehydrated mushrooms, wood ears, lily flowers, water, vegetable oil, sesame oil, rice wine, oyster sauce, sugar, salt, white pepper, grated ginger, and the white parts of the scallions. Mix until most of the liquid is absorbed, then marinate for at least 2 hours or overnight. Spread the mixture onto a shallow heatproof plate. Steam over high heat until the chicken is fully cooked and tender. Garnish with the green parts of the scallions and serve hot with steamed rice.
searchAliases: steamed chicken with dried lily flowers

strInstructions: Use freshly cooked or day-old rice and break up any large clumps before cooking. Beat the eggs with a pinch of salt. Heat oil in a wok over high heat, then add the beaten eggs and scramble until mostly set. Add the rice and stir-fry until the grains separate and heat through. Season with light soy sauce, white pepper, and salt to taste. Add scallions and continue tossing until evenly mixed. If the rice looks dry, sprinkle in a small amount of water or chicken stock to loosen the grains. Serve hot.

Prepare all ingredients before cooking. Dice the char siu pork, peel and devein the shrimp, beat the eggs, chop the green onions, thaw the vegetable mix, and loosen the leftover rice so the grains are separated. Heat 1/2 tablespoon of peanut oil in a wok or nonstick skillet over medium-high heat. Add the char siu pork and shrimp, then cook briefly until the shrimp turn white. Transfer them to a plate. Add 1 tablespoon of oil to the pan, then add the rice. Stir-fry until the grains are coated with oil and heated through. Pour in the light soy sauce and toss until evenly distributed. Push the rice to one side of the pan, add more oil to the empty side, then pour in the beaten eggs. Let the eggs set briefly, then scramble and mix them into the rice. Add the thawed vegetable mix and green onions, then season with salt and white pepper. Return the cooked shrimp and char siu pork to the pan, toss everything together, adjust seasoning if needed, and serve hot.
strIngredient4: Frozen Vegetable Mix or Green Peas

strInstructions: Cook chow mein noodles until just tender, then drain. Marinate sliced chicken with soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, cornstarch, and oil. Mix oyster sauce, soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sugar, and sesame oil for the sauce. Sear the chicken, then stir-fry cabbage, carrot, bean sprouts, garlic, and green onion. Add noodles and sauce, tossing until the noodles absorb the seasoning and the vegetables stay crisp.

Brown the beef pieces in oil, then set aside. Sauté ginger, garlic, and scallions until fragrant. Add the beef back to the pot with Shaoxing wine, light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, star anise, bay leaves, water, potatoes, and carrots. Bring to a boil, then reduce the heat and simmer until the beef is tender. Continue cooking until the potatoes soften and the sauce becomes rich. Serve hot with steamed rice.

Make a dough with flour, hot water, and salt, then rest until smooth. Roll into thin sheets, brush with oil, sprinkle with chopped scallions, and roll into coils. Flatten each coil into a pancake. Pan-fry in oil over medium heat until golden and crisp on both sides. Cut into wedges and serve hot with a soy-vinegar dipping sauce.

In a small saucepan over medium heat, add the neutral oil, Sichuan peppercorns, star anise, and garlic cloves. Heat for about 5 minutes until the garlic is lightly browned, then remove from the heat and let the infused oil cool. In a food processor, puree water, fresh ginger, and scallions until smooth. Add ground pork to a mixing bowl, then add the ginger-scallion puree in three batches, mixing in one direction until the liquid is fully absorbed each time. Add oyster sauce, light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar, white pepper, five spice powder, and salt, then mix until the filling becomes sticky and paste-like. In a separate bowl, combine finely chopped Chinese garlic chives with the strained infused oil, then mix them into the pork filling. Keep dumpling wrappers covered with a damp towel. Place about 1 tablespoon of filling in each wrapper, wet the edges, and pleat tightly to seal. Cook the dumplings by boiling, steaming, or pan-frying until the wrapper is tender and the pork filling is fully cooked. Serve with vinegar, chili sauce, or dumpling dipping sauce.

Prepare the aspic by simmering pork skin and bones with aromatics until a rich stock forms, then chill until firm. Mix ground pork with ginger, scallions, soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, sesame oil, sugar, and white pepper. Finely dice the chilled aspic and fold it into the pork filling. Place filling in thin dumpling wrappers, pleat tightly, and steam until the wrappers become translucent and the soup melts inside. Serve hot with black vinegar and ginger.
strMeasure5: 2 tbsp minced plus extra for serving

strInstructions: Finely chop the fish and mix it with ground pork, ginger, scallions, soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, sesame oil, salt, and white pepper until sticky. Place a spoonful of filling in each dumpling wrapper and seal tightly. Boil the dumplings in batches until they float and the filling is fully cooked. Serve with vinegar, soy sauce, and chili oil.

Cook noodles until springy, then drain. Brown ground pork with Sui Mi Ya Cai or preserved mustard greens. Mix sesame paste, soy sauce, Chinese black vinegar, chili oil, sugar, garlic, and noodle cooking water into a sauce. Place sauce in bowls, add noodles, top with pork mixture, scallions, and crushed peanuts if desired. Toss before eating.

Cook fresh wheat noodles and rinse briefly. Stir-fry pork belly or ground pork until lightly browned. Add ginger, sweet bean sauce, ground bean sauce, Shaoxing wine, and water, then simmer until thick and glossy. Serve the sauce over noodles with cucumber, carrot, bean sprouts, and scallions. Mix well before eating.

Simmer pork belly until firm but not falling apart, then cool and slice thinly. Stir-fry the slices until lightly browned and some fat renders. Add ginger, garlic, spicy bean paste, sweet bean sauce, and Shaoxing wine. Toss in leeks or green garlic and bell pepper, cooking until just tender. Serve with rice.

Blanch pork belly cubes to remove impurities, then drain. Melt rock sugar in oil until amber, then add pork and coat in caramel. Add Shaoxing wine, soy sauce, dark soy sauce, ginger, scallions, star anise, cinnamon, and water. Simmer gently until the pork is tender and the sauce reduces into a shiny glaze. Serve with steamed rice.

Beat eggs gently with warm water or stock and salt, avoiding too many bubbles. Strain the mixture into a shallow bowl. Cover with foil or a plate and steam over gentle heat until just set and silky. Rest briefly, then top with soy sauce, sesame oil, and scallions. Serve warm as a soft side dish.

If using smaller bok choy, cut each piece in half lengthwise, or into quarters if large. If using medium or large bok choy, separate the bulbs into individual stems, trim away the tough base, and cut very large stems diagonally into 1-inch pieces. Wash the bok choy thoroughly in a large bowl of cold water, agitating to remove sand and dirt. Soak for 5 to 10 minutes, then lift into a colander. Repeat washing until no dirt remains, then shake off excess water. Heat a wok or large pan over low heat and add the neutral oil and ginger. Add the garlic and let it sizzle for 10 to 15 seconds without browning. Add the bok choy and increase the heat to high. Stir-fry with a scooping motion until the oil and aromatics are evenly distributed. Add salt, sesame oil if using, MSG if using, and white pepper. Stir-fry for another 1 to 2 minutes until the bok choy is just tender. Add the cornstarch slurry to the center of the vegetables and toss until the bok choy is glossy. Plate and serve immediately.
strMeasure3: 1 slice, 1/8 inch thick and 2 inches long

strInstructions: Mix the chicken pieces with cornstarch, sesame paste, salt, and Shaoxing wine, then set aside for 20 minutes to marinate. In a separate bowl, mix all-purpose flour, toasted sesame seeds, and white pepper. Preheat the oven to 475°F or 250°C. Mix the marinated chicken again until there is no standing liquid, then dredge each piece in the flour and sesame mixture. Arrange the chicken on a parchment-lined baking sheet. Bake on the top rack for 8 minutes, then flip the pieces and broil on low for about 3 minutes until golden brown. Heat a wok over medium heat and add canola oil and minced garlic. Stir briefly, then add Shaoxing wine. After a few seconds, add chicken stock, rice wine vinegar, sugar, soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and sesame oil. Bring to a simmer, then gradually stir in the cornstarch slurry until the sauce thickens enough to coat a spoon. Add the baked chicken and chopped scallion, then toss until evenly coated. Garnish with toasted sesame seeds and serve.
strMeasure2: 1 tbsp for chicken plus 2 tbsp for slurry
strMeasure5: 1 tsp for chicken plus 1 tbsp for sauce

strInstructions: Cut the chicken into bite-size pieces and marinate with soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, cornstarch, and oil. Heat a wok and sear the chicken until lightly browned. Remove the chicken, then stir-fry ginger, garlic, and scallions until fragrant. Return the chicken to the wok with soy sauce, oyster sauce, sugar, and a splash of water or stock. Simmer briefly until the sauce thickens and coats the chicken. Serve hot with rice.

Soak sticky rice until hydrated, then steam or cook until tender. Stir-fry Chinese sausage, mushrooms, dried shrimp, scallions, and aromatics until fragrant. Add the cooked sticky rice and season with soy sauce, oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar, and white pepper. Toss until the grains are evenly coated and glossy. Serve hot as a dim sum-style rice dish.

Prepare a flaky pastry dough and chill it until firm. Roll the dough, cut into rounds, and press into tart molds. Mix eggs, sugar syrup, evaporated milk, and vanilla until smooth, then strain the custard. Fill each tart shell with custard. Bake until the pastry is golden and the custard is just set but still smooth. Cool slightly before serving.

Pat the fish dry and lightly sear both sides in hot oil. Remove or push aside, then stir-fry ginger, garlic, and scallions. Add Shaoxing wine, soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sugar, and water or stock. Simmer the fish gently, spooning sauce over the top until cooked through and flavorful. Reduce the sauce slightly and garnish with scallions.

strMeal: Steamed Fish with Ginger and Scallions
strInstructions: Clean and pat dry the whole fish or fillets. Place ginger and scallions under and over the fish, then steam until just cooked through. Discard excess liquid from the plate. Top with fresh scallion and ginger. Heat oil until shimmering and pour it over the aromatics. Finish with light soy sauce or seasoned soy sauce and serve immediately.

Blanch vegetables, tofu, noodles, and meats separately until almost cooked. Heat oil and stir-fry dried chilies, Sichuan peppercorns, ginger, garlic, and spicy hot pot base until fragrant. Add the prepared ingredients and toss thoroughly so everything is coated in the spicy numbing seasoning. Finish with scallions, cilantro, and sesame seeds.

Peel and dice the taro. Stir-fry Chinese sausage, dried shrimp, mushrooms, and scallions until fragrant. Cook the taro briefly, then combine with rice flour, glutinous rice flour, water, salt, sugar, and white pepper to form a thick batter. Transfer to a greased pan and steam until set. Cool completely, slice, and pan-fry until golden on both sides.

Marinate sliced pork with soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, cornstarch, and oil. Scramble eggs and set aside. Stir-fry pork, then add wood ear mushrooms, shiitake mushrooms, cabbage, carrot, and bamboo shoots. Season with soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper. Return the eggs and serve with thin pancakes and hoisin sauce.

Separate fresh rice noodles carefully. Marinate sliced beef with soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, cornstarch, and oil. Sear the beef quickly and remove. Stir-fry onion, scallions, and bean sprouts over high heat. Add noodles, light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and a little sugar. Return the beef and toss gently until the noodles are evenly colored and slightly smoky.

Mix sweet bean paste with water until smooth. Heat oil in a wok or skillet and cook ginger until fragrant. Add ground pork and stir-fry until browned. Pour in Shaoxing wine, then add the diluted bean paste. Simmer until the sauce thickens and becomes glossy. Stir in diced onion and cook until slightly softened. Cook the noodles separately, drain, and place in bowls. Top with the pork sauce and cucumber strips before serving.

In a large bowl, combine ground pork, chopped shrimp, light soy sauce, sesame oil, egg white, minced ginger, salt, sugar, and white pepper. Mix in one direction until the filling becomes sticky. Place about a teaspoon of filling in the center of each wonton wrapper, gather the wrapper around the filling, and pinch tightly to seal. In a separate pot, simmer homemade chicken stock with salt, ginger slices, and the white parts of green onions. Cook the Hong Kong-style wonton noodles in boiling water according to package instructions, then divide them into bowls. Cook the wontons in boiling water until they float, then continue cooking for about another minute. Add wontons to the noodle bowls, blanch choy sum or baby bok choy, then pour hot chicken soup over each bowl. Garnish with sliced green onion tops and fried shallots if desired.
strMeasure8: 3/4 tsp for filling plus 2 tsp for soup
strIngredient14: Choy Sum or Baby Bok Choy

strInstructions: Slice pork into thin matchsticks and marinate with Shaoxing wine, egg white, cornstarch, salt, and oil. Mix sweet bean paste, soy sauce, sugar, and water to make the sauce. Heat oil in a pan and quickly stir-fry the pork until just cooked, then remove. Sauté scallions until fragrant, add the sauce, and cook until thickened. Return the pork to the pan and toss until coated. Serve with sliced scallions, cucumber, tofu sheets, pancakes, or steamed rice.
searchAliases: shredded pork with sweet bean sauce

strInstructions: Combine flour and hot water to form a soft dough. Knead until smooth, cover, and let rest. Roll the dough into thin sheets, brush with oil, sprinkle with salt and chopped scallions, then roll and coil each piece. Flatten each coil into a pancake. Pan-fry in hot oil until both sides are golden brown and crisp. Serve warm with a soy-based dipping sauce.

Mix brown sugar, hot water, evaporated milk, eggs, melted butter or oil, flour, custard powder, baking powder, and yeast into a smooth batter. Let the batter rest until slightly bubbly. Pour into a lined cake pan and steam over medium-high heat until the cake rises and a toothpick comes out clean. Cool slightly before slicing.

Beat eggs with a pinch of salt. Heat oil in a wok and scramble the eggs until just set, then remove. Add more oil and cook tomatoes until softened and juicy. Season with sugar, salt, and a little soy sauce if desired. Return the eggs to the pan and gently fold everything together. Garnish with green onions and serve with rice.

Cut the firm tofu into thick blocks and carefully hollow a small pocket in each piece. In a bowl, combine ground pork, minced salted fish if using, minced ginger, Shaoxing wine, white pepper, salt, chopped scallion, oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, and cornstarch. Mix until the filling becomes sticky. Fill each tofu pocket with the pork mixture. Pan-fry the tofu, filling side down first, until browned, then turn gently. Add water or stock with oyster sauce, soy sauce, sugar, and a little cornstarch slurry to make a light sauce. Simmer briefly until the tofu is heated through and the pork filling is fully cooked. Serve hot.

Slice the beef against the grain into thin pieces. Add light soy sauce and Shaoxing wine, then mix until absorbed. Add cornstarch and mix again, then marinate for 15 minutes. In a small bowl, combine chicken broth, light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and sugar to make the sauce. Prepare the rice noodles by separating fresh noodles by hand, or cooking dried noodles until just chewy and tossing them with a little sesame oil. Heat oil in a large skillet or wok over high heat and sear the beef in a single layer until browned, then remove. Add the remaining oil, ginger, garlic, white onion, and the white parts of the green onions. Stir-fry until fragrant. Add the noodles and toss gently to coat with oil. Return the beef to the pan, pour in the sauce, and toss until evenly mixed. Add bean sprouts and cook until slightly softened. Add the green parts of the green onions and sesame oil, then toss a few more times. Serve hot.
strMeasure2: 1 tbsp for marinade plus 1 1/2 tbsp for sauce
strIngredient8: Dried Rice Noodles or Fresh Thick Rice Noodles

strInstructions: Mix hoisin sauce, honey, soy sauce, oyster sauce, Shaoxing wine, five-spice powder, garlic, and red fermented bean curd if using. Marinate pork strips for several hours or overnight. Roast the pork on a rack, brushing with marinade during cooking. Near the end, glaze with honey and roast until caramelized and slightly charred. Rest the meat before slicing.

Cut the pork belly into about 3/4-inch thick pieces. Add the pork to a medium pot, cover with water, bring to a boil, and boil for about 1 minute or until the pork turns opaque. Remove the pork, rinse it, discard the blanching water, and clean the pot. Over low heat, add the oil and sugar to the wok or pot. Melt the sugar slightly, then add the pork. Raise the heat to medium and cook until the pork is lightly browned. Lower the heat again, add the Shaoxing wine, and cook for about 2 minutes. Add the soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and water. Cover and simmer over medium heat for 45 minutes to 1 hour, stirring every 5 to 10 minutes and adding water if it gets too dry. Once the pork is fork-tender, uncover the wok if there is still a lot of liquid and stir continuously until the sauce reduces into a glossy coating.

Stir-fry ground pork with garlic, Shaoxing wine, soy sauce, and preserved mustard greens until browned and dry. In serving bowls, mix sesame paste, soy sauce, vinegar, chili oil, Sichuan peppercorn powder, sugar, and a little noodle water. Cook noodles until tender and blanch leafy greens. Add noodles to the sauce, top with the pork mixture and greens, then mix well before eating.

Simmer pork belly with ginger and scallions until just cooked, then cool and slice thinly. Heat oil in a wok and fry the pork slices until lightly browned and some fat renders out. Add doubanjiang and fermented black beans, then stir-fry until fragrant. Add leeks, peppers, soy sauce, and a little sugar. Toss until the vegetables are tender-crisp and the pork is coated in sauce.

Shred the pork tenderloin into thin strips. Add light soy sauce, salt, sugar, and ground white pepper, then mix well and let the pork rest for 15 minutes. Soak ginger and scallion slices in warm water, then add about 2 tablespoons of the scallion and ginger water to the pork and mix until absorbed. Mix cornstarch with water and add it to the pork. Before stir-frying, mix cooking oil into the pork shreds to help keep them separated. In a small bowl, combine light soy sauce, black vinegar, water, starch, sugar, and salt to make the Yu Xiang sauce. Heat a wok, add a small amount of oil, and quickly stir-fry the pork until it changes color, then remove. Leave the oil in the wok and fry doubanjiang and chopped pickled peppers over low heat until the oil turns red. Add chopped garlic and ginger and stir-fry until aromatic. Return the pork to the wok, stir the sauce again, then pour it in. Cook until the sauce boils and thickens. Add the Chinese scallion sections, mix well, and serve hot.
searchAliases: shredded pork in hot garlic sauce
strMeasure2: 1/4 tsp for marinade plus 2 tbsp for sauce
strMeasure4: 1 tbsp for marinade plus 2 tsp for sauce
strMeasure6: 1/4 tsp for marinade plus 1/4 tsp for sauce
strMeasure9: 1 tbsp for pork plus 3 tbsp for stir-frying
strMeasure14: 1 large, green part removed and cut into 1 cm sections

strInstructions: Soak mung bean vermicelli until pliable, then drain. Mix soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sugar, and water for the sauce. Stir-fry ground pork with garlic, ginger, and doubanjiang until fragrant and browned. Add the sauce and bring to a simmer. Add the noodles and toss until they absorb the sauce. Finish with green onions and sesame oil.

Slice lamb thinly and marinate with soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, cornstarch, and oil. Mix cumin, chili flakes, and salt in a small bowl. Heat a wok until very hot and sear the lamb quickly until browned. Add onion, garlic, and dried chilies, then stir-fry until fragrant. Sprinkle in the cumin spice mix and toss well. Add cilantro or green onions before serving.

strMeal: Dim Sum Steamed Spare Ribs with Black Beans
strInstructions: Wash the pork ribs in cold water and soak for about 10 minutes to remove bone fragments and excess blood. Drain well. Place the ribs in a bowl with sugar, sea salt, clear rice wine or Shaoxing wine, sesame oil, and white pepper. Mix until evenly coated, then cover and marinate overnight for best flavor, or at least 30 minutes if short on time. Add cornstarch and water to the marinated ribs and mix until the ribs are lightly coated with almost no extra liquid. Arrange the ribs on a shallow heatproof plate. Sprinkle the chopped green peppers, red peppers, and rinsed fermented black beans over the top. Steam over simmering water for about 20 minutes, or until the ribs are opaque and cooked through. Serve as dim sum or over steamed rice.
searchAliases: steamed spare ribs with black beans
strIngredient4: Clear Rice Wine or Shaoxing Wine

strInstructions: Pat shrimp very dry and lightly coat with cornstarch. Fry or shallow-fry until the shells are crisp and the shrimp are cooked, then drain. In a clean wok, sauté garlic, chilies, and green onions until fragrant. Add the shrimp back to the wok and sprinkle with salt, white pepper, and five-spice powder. Toss quickly and serve immediately.

Combine soy sauce, dark soy sauce, rock sugar, Shaoxing wine, ginger, scallions, star anise, cinnamon, and water in a pot. Bring to a simmer until the sugar dissolves. Lower the chicken into the liquid and gently poach, turning occasionally so the color is even. Continue cooking until the chicken is done and tender. Let it rest before chopping. Spoon some sauce over the chicken before serving.

Combine ground pork with ginger, scallions, soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, sesame oil, egg, and cornstarch. Mix until sticky, then form large meatballs. Brown the meatballs in oil until the outside is set. Place cabbage in a pot, add broth and seasonings, then nestle the meatballs on top. Simmer gently until the meatballs are cooked through and tender. Serve with the cabbage and broth.

Add sesame oil, vegetable oil, ginger, garlic, and dried chili to a wok over medium heat. Let the aromatics infuse the oil for a couple of minutes. Increase the heat to high, add the chicken wings in a single layer, and sear until golden brown on both sides. Add warm water, Shaoxing wine, dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, and sugar. Stir, cover, and simmer over medium heat for about 15 minutes, or 20 minutes if you want the chicken more tender. Remove the cover and raise the heat to reduce the sauce until it becomes thick, dark, and glossy. Stir often to prevent burning. Add Thai basil leaves or scallions and cook for another minute until wilted. Serve hot with rice.
